text: Synopsis Affordable hydrogen (H2) production from biomass remains an urgent goal for sustainable energy and bio-refinery industries. Recent studies show that non-classical anodic H2 production from 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) at a low overpotential (around 0.1V vs RHE) is a very efficient approach that not only reduces the energy consumption, but also turns a resourceful bio-based feedstock into useful commodity chemicals. However, the reaction pathways remain unclear in this fascinating process. In this study, by integration of in situ Raman and Infrared spectroscopy, isotope experiment and density functional theory calculations, the mechanisms of the whole process were fully clarified, revealing two pairs of inter-conversion reversible gem-diol species (5-(Dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM−) and dianion (DHMFM2−)). At the basic condition, the formed DHMFM2− anion species can proceed to produce H2 via hydrogen reduction through the H− transfer. In contrast, the oxidation of other gem-diol species (DHMFM−) leads to H2O evolution through H+ reduction. An increase in overpotential can greatly accelerate the oxidation reaction rate of DHMFM−, causing the shift of the competitive equilbrium between DHMFM− and DHMFM2− towards DHMFM− at the expense of anodic H2 production until cessation. This study captured the elusive gem-diol anion species (DHMFM2−) species leading to the bio-conversion of aldehyde to H2, which is crucial to the design and improvement of the bifunctional catalysts.
